articles.title: Bronze Statue Honors Monty Python's Terry Jones
articles.summary: A bronze statue of Terry Jones, the beloved Monty Python actor and writer, will be unveiled in Colwyn Bay, Wales, this weekend. Jones, who died in 2020 from frontotemporal dementia, is depicted as the 'nude organist,' a recurring character from Monty Python's Flying Circus. The life-sized sculpture, created by artist Nick Elphick, shows Jones naked except for a flapping tie, with wild hair and a grin, playing an organ that merges into a writing desk. The desk includes references to his many achievements, such as his medieval history books. The project was funded by a campaign that raised £120,000 in six months, supported by fellow Pythons Michael Palin, Terry Gilliam, John Cleese, and Eric Idle, as well as celebrities like Emma Thompson and Steve Coogan. Palin noted that the tribute would have made Jones laugh. Elphick spent 18 months working on the statue, aiming to capture Jones's larger-than-life personality while ensuring the smile pleased his family. The unveiling on Saturday will be attended by Palin and Gilliam.

articles.title: TikTok's BookTok Community Launches Official Bestseller List
articles.summary: In recent years, TikTok has become a powerful force in the book industry. The #BookTok community, with over 77 million posts, has transformed how readers discover and discuss books. Now, this influence is formalized with the first official UK BookTok bestseller list, compiled by Media Control and NielsenIQ BookData. The list combines UK sales data with analysis of the #BookTok hashtag, updating monthly. The inaugural top 20 is entirely female authors, with Irish writer Chloe Walsh appearing most frequently—all six books in her Boys of Tommen series made the list. The chart also highlights how BookTok revitalizes older titles; Donna Tartt's The Secret History, published in 1992, gained new popularity. Many listed books are upcoming screen adaptations, such as Colleen Hoover's It Ends With Us and Rebecca Yarros's Fourth Wing. The dominant genre is romantasy (romance and fantasy), with books by Sarah J Maas and Rebecca Yarros. BookTok influencers Kris and Mads note that romantasy is accessible and builds engaged fanbases. The list reflects that younger readers are more influenced by social media recommendations than traditional media. The influence of creators can cause immediate sales spikes, as seen when a self-published series sold out after their recommendation.

articles.title: Touring Costs Crush Small Bands; Harry Styles Fund Offers Relief
articles.summary: For up-and-coming bands like Brown Horse from Norwich, touring is financially draining. They often earn more from selling merchandise than from ticket sales, leading them to joke about being traveling T-shirt salesmen. To cut costs, they sleep on friends' floors and drive an old van. The band members also hold day jobs and sacrifice their well-being to continue touring. However, a new initiative is providing some relief. A £1 levy on tickets for arena shows by major artists like Harry Styles, Foo Fighters, and Florence + the Machine has raised £5 million for the charity The Live Trust. The first £500,000 is being distributed, with £125,000 allocated to musicians. Brown Horse received nearly £5,000, which they plan to use for accommodation, keeping ticket prices affordable, and paying themselves for the first time in a year. This support is crucial for sustaining grassroots music and helping small bands continue their careers.

articles.title: Disruptive Behavior at Cinemas: Is It Getting Worse?
articles.summary: A BBC article investigates whether cinema etiquette is declining, based on personal experiences and interviews. The author recounts multiple incidents: during a screening of 'Project Hail Mary,' a person translated dialogue in real-time, others scrolled on phones, and a family unwrapped a full picnic. At a Valentine's Day showing of 'Wuthering Heights,' a woman ate a chocolate heart with a noisy wrapper. These disruptions, though met with shushing or complaints, still broke the immersion. Film TikToker Cerys Hawkes shares tips to avoid disturbances, such as choosing earlier showtimes or arthouse cinemas. She describes a severe incident during 'Smile 2' where two men filmed themselves with flash on, shouting throughout. They only stopped when staff intervened, but resumed later. Hawkes links disruptive behavior to messiness, citing 'Minecraft Movie' screenings where people threw popcorn and even brought a live chicken. The article suggests that while cinemas enforce rules through announcements, behavior may be worsening, and audiences are increasingly frustrated.

articles.title: BYD Thrives Without US Market, Focuses on Global Expansion
articles.summary: Chinese electric vehicle (EV) manufacturer BYD has stated that it can succeed without the US market, as it experiences surging demand in other regions. The company, which overtook Tesla as the world's largest EV seller in 2023, is capitalizing on rising fuel prices driven by geopolitical tensions. BYD's executive vice president Stella Li highlighted that consumers are turning to EVs to save money on fuel. The company is struggling to keep up with demand, particularly in Brazil, the UK, and Europe. BYD is investing in flash charging technology, which can add hundreds of kilometers of range in minutes, addressing a key barrier to EV adoption. At the Beijing Auto Show, Chinese carmakers showcased their latest models, emphasizing technological advancements in batteries, charging, and software. However, Chinese EV-makers face tariffs and regulatory scrutiny in the US, which has criticized Chinese subsidies and raised national security concerns. Li expressed confidence that BYD's brand recognition is growing globally, and the company is competing on technology rather than just price.

articles.title: Norrie Overcomes Machac in Madrid Thriller
articles.summary: British tennis player Cameron Norrie defeated Tomas Machac of the Czech Republic in a three-set battle at the Madrid Open. The match ended 6-2, 6-7(6), 7-6(5) after nearly three hours. Norrie dominated the first set, breaking Machac's serve early and winning 6-2 in 36 minutes. However, Machac fought back in the second set, saving a match point in the tie-break to win 8-6. In the deciding set, Norrie trailed by a break and faced defeat as Machac served for the match. But Norrie broke back and eventually won the tie-break 7-5. Norrie described it as one of the favorite matches of his career, praising the atmosphere and the challenge. He will face either Tommy Paul or Thiago Agustin Tirante in the next round. This victory continues Norrie's good form after reaching the Barcelona Open quarter-finals last week. Elsewhere, British number one Katie Boulter lost to world number five Jessica Pegula 6-4, 6-4, and world number one Jannik Sinner beat Benjamin Bonzi in three sets.
